tier_1_regimens.site: Renal medullary carcinoma (RMC) or collecting duct carcinoma
tier_1_regimens.setting: First-line (preferred outside of clinical trials)

biomarkers.marker: Renal medullary carcinoma or collecting duct histology

trial_data.n_patients: 23 (for gemcitabine/cisplatin or carboplatin in collecting duct)
trial_data.os_data: Collecting duct: Median OS was 10.5 months

tier_1_regimens.notes: For patients with RMC or collecting duct carcinoma. Partial responses have been observed with cytotoxic chemotherapy (gemcitabine/carboplatin, paclitaxel/carboplatin, or gemcitabine/cisplatin) and other platinum-based chemotherapies. Doxorubicin/gemcitabine can also produce responses in RMC. Oral anti-angiogenic targeted therapies generally do not produce responses in patients with RMC. Erlotinib + bevacizumab can produce responses even in heavily pretreated patients with RMC. Immune checkpoint therapies (ipilimumab + nivolumab or pembrolizumab) should be avoided in RMC outside of a clinical trial as they have demonstrated limited efficacy and may be associated with rapid disease progression. Outside of clinical trials, platinum-based chemotherapy regimens should be the preferred first-line therapy for RMC. [Update, 227, 228, 229]

tier_2_handbook.definition: Renal cell carcinoma (RCC) is a cancer that originates from the renal tubular epithelium of the kidney. It is a heterogeneous group of malignancies arising from the renal parenchyma, distinct from urothelial carcinoma of the renal pelvis. The embryological origin is from the metanephric mesenchyme. The anatomical boundaries are defined by the kidney and its surrounding structures, with staging determined by the extent of the primary tumor (T), involvement of regional lymph nodes (N), and presence of distant metastases (M). The most common presentation is as a solid renal mass, often discovered incidentally on imaging [MS-4].

subtypes.name: Clear Cell Renal Cell Carcinoma (ccRCC)
subtypes.description: The most common histologic subtype, characterized by cells with clear cytoplasm due to lipid and glycogen accumulation.
subtypes.frequency: Approximately 80% to 90% of all RCC cases [MS-4].
subtypes.key_associations: Associated with VHL gene mutations and loss of 3p.

subtypes.name: Papillary Renal Cell Carcinoma
subtypes.description: The second most common subtype, with tumor cells arranged in papillary or tubulopapillary structures.
subtypes.key_associations: Can be hereditary (HPRC) associated with MET mutations.

subtypes.name: Chromophobe Renal Cell Carcinoma
subtypes.description: Cells with prominent cell membranes and pale cytoplasm.
subtypes.key_associations: Can be part of Birt-Hogg-Dubé syndrome.

subtypes.name: Collecting Duct Carcinoma
subtypes.description: An aggressive, rare subtype originating from the collecting ducts of the renal medulla.

subtypes.name: Renal Medullary Carcinoma (RMC)
subtypes.description: A rare and aggressive variant associated with sickle-cell trait or disease.
subtypes.frequency: Approximately 2% of all primary renal tumors in young people [MS-30].
subtypes.key_associations: Almost exclusively in patients with sickle-cell trait or hemoglobin sickle cell disease.

subtypes.name: Translocation RCC (TFE3/TFEB-altered)
subtypes.description: Molecularly defined subtypes characterized by gene rearrangements involving TFE3 or TFEB.

subtypes.name: Succinate Dehydrogenase (SDH)-Deficient RCC
subtypes.description: A molecularly defined subtype associated with SDH gene mutations.
subtypes.key_associations: Can be hereditary as part of PGL/PCC syndrome.

subtypes.name: FH-Deficient RCC (including HLRCC-associated RCC)
subtypes.description: A molecularly defined subtype associated with fumarate hydratase (FH) mutations.
subtypes.key_associations: Can be hereditary as part of HLRCC syndrome.

subtypes.name: BAP1-Tumor Predisposition Syndrome (TPDS)-associated RCC
subtypes.description: A molecularly defined subtype associated with BAP1 gene mutations.

tier_2_handbook.molecular_pathogenesis: The molecular pathogenesis of RCC is complex and varies by subtype. For clear cell RCC (ccRCC), the key genomic event is inactivation of the von Hippel-Lindau (VHL) tumor suppressor gene on chromosome 3p, leading to activation of the hypoxia-inducible factor (HIF) pathway and overexpression of vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) and platelet-derived growth factor (PDGF) [MS-4]. Other important alterations include mutations in PBRM1, SETD2, and BAP1 genes. For papillary RCC, mutations in the MET proto-oncogene are common in hereditary forms. TFE3 and TFEB gene rearrangements define translocation RCC subtypes. SDH-deficient RCC is characterized by germline mutations in SDH genes (SDHA, SDHB, SDHC, SDHD). FH-deficient RCC is caused by germline mutations in the FH gene. BAP1-TPDS-associated RCC involves mutations in the BAP1 gene [MS-32, MS-34].

epidemiology.incidence: An estimated 80,450 Americans will be diagnosed with cancers of the kidney and renal pelvis in 2026 [MS-4].
epidemiology.mortality: An estimated 15,160 Americans will die from the disease in 2026 [MS-4].
epidemiology.trends: In the United States, there has been a 0.9% increase in kidney cancer cases and a 1.6% decrease in mortality [MS-4].
epidemiology.demographics: Cancers of the kidney and renal pelvis comprise approximately 4% of all new cancers. The median age at diagnosis is 65 years [MS-4]. About 66% of cases are diagnosed as localized disease, with a 5-year survival rate of 79% [MS-4].

risk_factors.detail: Tobacco smoking is an established risk factor for RCC development [MS-4].
Obesity is an established risk factor for RCC development [MS-4].
Hypertension is an established risk factor for RCC development [MS-4].
A family history of RCC may indicate an inherited predisposition, such as a hereditary RCC syndrome [MS-5].
Several hereditary syndromes predispose to RCC, including von Hippel-Lindau (VHL) disease, hereditary leiomyomatosis and renal cell cancer (HLRCC), Birt-Hogg-Dubé syndrome, and others [MS-4, MS-32].
Almost exclusively associated with renal medullary carcinoma [MS-30].

protective_factors: None explicitly mentioned in the source text.
tier_2_handbook.initial_workup: The initial workup for a patient with a suspicious kidney mass includes a history and physical (H&P), complete blood count (CBC) with differential, comprehensive metabolic panel, urinalysis, abdomen/pelvis CT or MRI, and chest CT (preferred) or chest x-ray [KID-1]. If clinically indicated, a bone scan or brain MRI may be performed. A core needle biopsy may be considered for small lesions to confirm malignancy and guide treatment decisions [KID-1, footnote c]. If urothelial carcinoma is suspected, urine cytology, ureteroscopy, or percutaneous biopsy should be considered. For patients with multiple renal masses, age ≤46 years, or family history, genetic evaluation for hereditary RCC syndromes should be considered [KID-1].

stage_I_T1a.primary_treatment: Partial nephrectomy (preferred) is the recommended treatment. Other options include percutaneous ablation, stereotactic body radiation therapy (SBRT) for non-optimal surgical candidates, active surveillance, or radical nephrectomy in select patients [KID-1, KID-A].
stage_I_T1a.follow_up: Follow-up is individualized. For active surveillance: H&P annually, laboratory tests annually as clinically indicated, abdomen imaging (CT, MRI, or US) within 6 months and then annually, and repeat chest imaging if intervention is considered [KID-C]. For post-ablation: H&P annually, lab tests annually, abdomen imaging with contrast at 1-3 months, 6 months, 12 months, then annually; chest CT annually for 5 years [KID-C]. For post-SBRT: abdomen/chest CT every 3 months for year 1, every 6 months for year 2, every 9 months for years 3-4, and annually for year 5; renal function evaluation on same schedule [KID-C]. For post-nephrectomy: H&P annually, lab tests annually, baseline abdomen CT or MRI within 3-12 months, then annually for up to 5 years; chest CT annually for at least 5 years [KID-C].

stage_I_T1b.primary_treatment: Partial nephrectomy or radical nephrectomy. Active surveillance, percutaneous ablation (category 2B), or SBRT are options for select patients [KID-1, KID-A].
stage_I_T1b.follow_up: Same follow-up recommendations as for Stage I (T1a) disease [KID-C].

stage_II.primary_treatment: Partial nephrectomy or radical nephrectomy. SBRT may be considered in select patients (category 2B) [KID-2].
stage_II.adjuvant_treatment: For clear cell histology: surveillance, adjuvant pembrolizumab (category 1), or adjuvant belzutifan + pembrolizumab for Grade 4 tumors with clear cell histology ± sarcomatoid features [KID-2]. For non-clear cell histology: surveillance [KID-2].
stage_II.follow_up: H&P annually, laboratory tests annually, abdomen/pelvis CT or MRI every 6 months for 2 years, then annually for up to 5 years; chest CT annually for at least 5 years [KID-C].

stage_III.primary_treatment: Radical nephrectomy or partial nephrectomy if clinically indicated. SBRT may be considered in select patients (category 3) [KID-2].
stage_III.adjuvant_treatment: For clear cell histology: adjuvant pembrolizumab (category 1) or adjuvant belzutifan + pembrolizumab. For non-clear cell histology: surveillance or clinical trial [KID-2].
stage_III.follow_up: H&P every 3-6 months for 3 years, then annually for up to 5 years. Comprehensive metabolic panel and other tests every 3-6 months for 3 years, then annually for up to 5 years. Abdomen/pelvis CT or MRI within 3-6 months, then every 3-6 months for at least 3 years, then annually for up to 5 years. Chest CT within 3-6 months, then every 3-6 months for at least 3 years, then annually for up to 5 years. Additional imaging (bone scan, brain imaging) as symptoms warrant. FDG-PET useful in certain circumstances (e.g., FH-deficient or SDHB-deficient RCC) [KID-C].

stage_IV_resectable_T4_M0.primary_treatment: Nephrectomy followed by adjuvant treatment. For clear cell histology: adjuvant pembrolizumab or adjuvant belzutifan + pembrolizumab. For non-clear cell histology: surveillance or clinical trial [KID-3].
stage_IV_resectable_T4_M0.follow_up: Same as for Stage III disease [KID-C].

clear_cell_histology.first_line_therapy: Clinical trial, or first-line systemic therapy (category 1) [KID-4, KID-D]. Preferred options include axitinib + pembrolizumab, cabozantinib + nivolumab, ipilimumab + nivolumab, lenvatinib + pembrolizumab, and cabozantinib (for poor-/intermediate-risk) [KID-D]. Other recommended options include axitinib + avelumab, pazopanib, sunitinib. Useful in certain circumstances: active surveillance (for select asymptomatic patients), axitinib [KID-D].
clear_cell_histology.subsequent_therapy: Clinical trial or subsequent therapy based on prior IO therapy status [KID-4, KID-D]. For IO therapy naïve: axitinib + pembrolizumab, cabozantinib, cabozantinib + nivolumab, everolimus/lenvatinib, ipilimumab + nivolumab, lenvatinib + pembrolizumab, nivolumab. For prior IO therapy: axitinib, belzutifan, cabozantinib, everolimus/lenvatinib, tivozanib [KID-D].

non_clear_cell_histology.systemic_therapy: Clinical trial (preferred) or systemic therapy (category 1) [KID-4, KID-D]. Preferred options include cabozantinib, cabozantinib + nivolumab, lenvatinib + pembrolizumab. Other recommended options include erlotinib + bevacizumab (for selected patients with papillary RCC including HLRCC), everolimus/lenvatinib, ipilimumab + nivolumab, nivolumab, pembrolizumab, sunitinib. Useful in certain circumstances: axitinib, everolimus, everolimus + bevacizumab [KID-D]. For collecting duct or renal medullary carcinoma, platinum-based chemotherapy regimens are preferred first-line [KID-D, footnote i].

stage_IV_M1_or_relapsed.follow_up: H&P every 6-16 weeks for patients receiving systemic therapy. Laboratory evaluation as per requirements for therapeutic agent. Chest, abdomen, pelvis CT or MRI every 6-16 weeks as per physician discretion. Consider head MRI at baseline and as clinically indicated. Bone scan/bone survey as clinically indicated. FDG-PET useful in certain circumstances (e.g., bone-predominant disease, prior to metastasectomy, FH-deficient or SDHB-deficient RCC) [KID-C].

first_line_therapy_clear_cell.preferred: Axitinib + pembrolizumab (category 1), cabozantinib + nivolumab (category 1), ipilimumab + nivolumab (category 1), lenvatinib + pembrolizumab (category 1), cabozantinib (category 2A for poor-/intermediate-risk) [KID-D].

io_therapy_naïve.other_recommended: Axitinib + pembrolizumab, cabozantinib, cabozantinib + nivolumab, everolimus/lenvatinib, ipilimumab + nivolumab, lenvatinib + pembrolizumab, nivolumab [KID-D].
io_therapy_naïve.useful_in_certain_circumstances: Axitinib, everolimus, lenvatinib, pazopanib, sunitinib, tivozanib, belzutifan, bevacizumab [KID-D].

prior_io_therapy.useful_in_certain_circumstances: Everolimus, ipilimumab + nivolumab, lenvatinib, pazopanib, sunitinib, axitinib + pembrolizumab, bevacizumab, cabozantinib + nivolumab, lenvatinib + pembrolizumab [KID-D].

systemic_therapy_non_clear_cell.preferred: Clinical trial, cabozantinib, cabozantinib + nivolumab, lenvatinib + pembrolizumab [KID-D].
systemic_therapy_non_clear_cell.other_recommended: Erlotinib + bevacizumab (for selected patients with papillary RCC including HLRCC), everolimus/lenvatinib, ipilimumab + nivolumab, nivolumab, pembrolizumab, sunitinib [KID-D].
systemic_therapy_non_clear_cell.special_considerations: For collecting duct or renal medullary carcinoma (RMC), platinum-based chemotherapy regimens (e.g., gemcitabine/carboplatin, paclitaxel/carboplatin, gemcitabine/cisplatin) are preferred first-line. Immune checkpoint therapies should be avoided in RMC outside of a clinical trial [KID-D, footnote i].

hereditary_RCC_syndromes.overview: Hereditary RCC accounts for about 3% of all RCC cases. Seven major syndromes are covered: BAP1-TPDS, Birt-Hogg-Dubé syndrome (BHDS), HLRCC, hereditary papillary renal carcinoma (HPRC), hereditary paraganglioma/pheochromocytoma (PGL/PCC) syndrome, tuberous sclerosis complex (TSC), and von Hippel-Lindau (VHL) disease [MS-32, HERED-RCC-2].
hereditary_RCC_syndromes.genetic_testing_and_surveillance: Individuals with a personal or family history of an RCC syndrome should undergo genetic risk evaluation. Screening recommendations vary by syndrome (e.g., abdomen MRI or CT starting at different ages and intervals) [HERED-RCC-1, HERED-RCC-B].
hereditary_RCC_syndromes.surgical_recommendations: Nephron-sparing surgery is preferred for syndromes with multifocal/bilateral tumors (e.g., BHDS, HPRC, TSC). Radical nephrectomy is recommended for aggressive syndromes like HLRCC [HERED-RCC-C].
hereditary_RCC_syndromes.systemic_therapy: Syndrome-specific recommendations include belzutifan for VHL-associated RCC, everolimus for TSC-associated angiomyolipoma, and erlotinib + bevacizumab for HLRCC-associated RCC [HERED-RCC-D].

clinical_features.typical_presentation: Patients with renal cell carcinoma (RCC) typically present with a suspicious renal mass identified incidentally on imaging, as increased use of imaging methods including abdomen CT with or without pelvis CT and MRI has led to more frequent incidental detection of RCC [MS-4]. The classic triad of hematuria, flank mass, and flank pain is now less commonly seen at diagnosis compared to historical presentations [MS-4]. Among the approximately 80,450 Americans estimated to be diagnosed with cancers of the kidney and renal pelvis in 2026, approximately 66% are diagnosed as localized disease, and the 5-year survival rate between 2015 and 2021 is 79% [1, MS-4]. The median age at diagnosis is 65 years [2, MS-4]. RCC comprises approximately 85% of kidney tumors, with approximately 80% to 90% having clear cell histology (ccRCC) [17-20, MS-4]. Less common histologies include papillary, oncocytic and chromophobe, TFE3-rearranged and TFEB-altered (translocation) RCC, collecting duct carcinoma, and SMARCB1-deficient medullary renal carcinoma [21-23, MS-4]. Hereditary RCC syndromes account for approximately 3% of all RCC cases and should be suspected in younger patients (≤46 years), those with bilateral or multifocal tumors, or those with a family history [242, MS-32].

symptoms.name: Hematuria
symptoms.detail: Classically described as part of the triad of hematuria, flank mass, and flank pain, though now less common at presentation due to incidental detection [MS-4].
symptoms.frequency: Classic symptom; less commonly seen at diagnosis in the current era of incidental imaging detection [MS-4].

symptoms.name: Flank pain
symptoms.detail: Classically described as part of the triad of hematuria, flank mass, and flank pain, though now less common at presentation due to incidental detection [MS-4].
symptoms.frequency: Classic symptom; less commonly seen at diagnosis in the current era of incidental imaging detection [MS-4].

symptoms.name: Flank mass
symptoms.detail: Classically described as part of the triad of hematuria, flank mass, and flank pain, though now less common at presentation due to incidental detection [MS-4].
symptoms.frequency: Classic symptom; less commonly seen at diagnosis in the current era of incidental imaging detection [MS-4].

symptoms.name: Bone pain
symptoms.detail: May indicate bone metastases; bone metastasis occurs in approximately 30% to 40% of patients with advanced RCC [14, 233, 235]. Most bone and brain metastases are symptomatic at diagnosis [34, MS-4].
symptoms.frequency: Presenting symptom in patients with metastatic disease [MS-4]; bone metastases in 30%-40% of advanced cases.

symptoms.name: Constitutional symptoms
symptoms.detail: Fever, weight loss, and anemia may occur as presenting features of RCC [MS-4].

symptoms.name: Pulmonary symptoms
symptoms.detail: Pulmonary symptoms attributable to lung parenchyma or mediastinal metastases may be the presenting feature; lung metastases occur in 50% to 60% of patients with relapse [MS-4, 103].
symptoms.frequency: Presenting symptom in patients with metastatic disease; most common site of distant recurrence [MS-4].

symptoms.name: Adenopathy
symptoms.detail: Lymphadenopathy may be the presenting feature of metastatic RCC [MS-4].
symptoms.frequency: Presenting symptom in patients with metastatic disease [MS-4].

symptoms.name: Varicocele
symptoms.detail: A varicocele may be an unusual presenting sign of RCC [MS-4].

signs.name: Incidental renal mass on imaging
signs.detail: As the use of imaging methods (eg, abdomen CT with or without pelvis CT, MRI) has become more widespread, the frequency of incidental detection of RCC has increased [MS-4].
signs.frequency: Most common mode of presentation in the current era [MS-4].

signs.name: Suspicious mass on CT or MRI
signs.detail: The initial workup includes abdomen ± pelvis CT or MRI performed with and without contrast, such as a renal protocol for the abdomen [KID-1, MS-5].

signs.name: Palpable abdominal/flank mass
signs.detail: A palpable mass may be detected on physical examination, though less common with contemporary incidental detection [MS-4].
signs.frequency: Less commonly detected on physical examination at presentation [MS-4].

atypical_or_missed: Incidental detection on imaging for unrelated conditions is now the most common mode of presentation, often without any referable symptoms [MS-4].

Hereditary RCC syndromes may present with bilateral or multifocal tumors at a younger age, which can delay recognition of the hereditary etiology if not suspected [MS-32]. VHL-associated tumors, HLRCC, BHDS, and other syndromes have systemic manifestations beyond the kidney that may be missed [HERED-RCC-2, MS-32].
Central renal masses may mimic urothelial carcinoma; if so, urine cytology, ureteroscopy, or percutaneous mass biopsy should be considered [MS-5].
Homogeneous infiltration of renal parenchyma should prompt evaluation to rule out lymphoma [MS-5].

Renal medullary carcinoma (RMC) and collecting duct carcinoma are rare aggressive variants that may present at advanced stages with metastatic disease (67%-95% for RMC at presentation) and require different treatment approaches [220-222, 223-226, MS-30].

red_flags: Hematuria (visible or microscopic) — warrants complete evaluation including imaging and potentially urological assessment [KID-1, MS-4]
Flank mass detected on physical examination — suspicious for renal malignancy [MS-4]
Flank pain with or without hematuria — requires imaging evaluation [MS-4]
Bone pain with or without elevated serum alkaline phosphatase (ALP) — suggests possible bone metastases; bone scan may be indicated [34, MS-4]
Pulmonary symptoms (cough, dyspnea) — may indicate lung metastases; most common site of distant recurrence in 50% to 60% of relapsed patients [103, MS-4]
Lymphadenopathy on examination or imaging — requires further evaluation for regional or distant metastatic disease [MS-4]
Fever, weight loss, anemia — constitutional symptoms that may indicate advanced or metastatic disease [MS-4]
Neurologic symptoms — may indicate brain metastases; brain MRI should be performed [MS-4, MS-31]
Bilateral or multifocal renal masses in a young patient (≤46 years) or patient with family history — raises suspicion for hereditary RCC syndrome and warrants genetic evaluation [HERED-RCC-1, MS-33]
Suspected or confirmed VHL or PGL/PCC syndrome — patients are at increased risk for pheochromocytomas and should have blood and/or urine screening prior to any surgical procedure [HERED-RCC-C]

RMC or collecting duct histology — extremely aggressive variants requiring platinum-based chemotherapy as preferred first-line therapy; immune checkpoint therapies should be avoided outside clinical trials due to limited efficacy and potential for rapid progression [KID-D 3 of 3, MS-30]

diagnostic.rationale: To evaluate symptoms including hematuria, flank mass, flank pain, bone pain, constitutional symptoms, and to assess performance status; to obtain family history suggesting hereditary RCC syndromes [KID-1, MS-4].
diagnostic.key_findings: Classic triad of hematuria, flank mass, and flank pain (now less common); signs of metastatic disease; features suggesting hereditary syndromes (bilateral or multifocal tumors, young age, cutaneous findings) [MS-4, HERED-RCC-1].

diagnostic.rationale: To evaluate for anemia, polycythemia (paraneoplastic erythropoietin production), or leukocytosis; part of the standard initial workup [KID-1, MS-5].
diagnostic.key_findings: Anemia may indicate chronic disease or bleeding; polycythemia may be paraneoplastic; leukocytosis may occur [KID-1].

diagnostic.rationale: To assess renal function (serum creatinine), liver function (LFTs), serum corrected calcium (which may be elevated as a paraneoplastic manifestation or bone metastases marker), and electrolytes; part of standard initial workup [KID-1, MS-5].
diagnostic.key_findings: Elevated calcium (hypercalcemia) may be paraneoplastic or from bone metastases; elevated LDH is a poor prognostic factor in metastatic disease [KID-E, MS-14].

diagnostic.rationale: To evaluate for microscopic or gross hematuria; part of the standard initial workup [KID-1, MS-5].
diagnostic.key_findings: Hematuria may indicate renal mass or other urological pathology [KID-1].

diagnostic.test: Abdomen ± pelvis CT or MRI with and without IV contrast (renal protocol)
diagnostic.rationale: Essential initial imaging to detect, characterize, and stage the renal mass; CT with and without contrast (renal protocol) is strongly preferred; MRI can evaluate the inferior vena cava if tumor involvement is suspected and can be used instead of CT when contrast cannot be administered due to allergy or moderate renal insufficiency [KID-1, MS-5].
diagnostic.key_findings: Renal mass characterization (size, location, enhancement pattern); local extent of disease; regional lymph node involvement; invasion into renal vein or IVC; adrenal gland involvement [KID-1, ST-1].

diagnostic.rationale: Essential for staging to evaluate for pulmonary metastases, the most common site of distant recurrence [KID-1, MS-5].
diagnostic.key_findings: Pulmonary nodules or masses indicating metastatic disease; lung is the most common site of metastasis [MS-4].

diagnostic.rationale: Performed when clinically indicated — elevated serum alkaline phosphatase (ALP) or bone pain — to evaluate for osseous metastases [KID-1, MS-4].
diagnostic.key_findings: Osteolytic lesions typical of RCC bone metastases [MS-31].

diagnostic.rationale: Performed when clinically indicated if neurologic signs, presentation, or symptoms suggest brain metastases [KID-1, MS-4].
diagnostic.key_findings: Brain metastases occur in approximately 2% to 8% of patients with advanced RCC [233, MS-31].

staging.test: Abdomen/pelvis CT or MRI with and without contrast
staging.rationale: Primary imaging modality for staging local extent of disease, regional lymph nodes, venous involvement (renal vein, IVC), and adrenal glands [KID-1, MS-5].
staging.indication: All patients with suspected or confirmed renal mass [KID-1].

staging.rationale: Essential staging study to evaluate for pulmonary metastases [KID-1, MS-5].
staging.indication: All patients with suspected or confirmed renal mass [KID-1].

staging.rationale: Preferred for evaluation of inferior vena cava (IVC) when tumor involvement is suspected; can be used instead of CT for detecting renal masses and staging when contrast cannot be administered [MS-5].
staging.indication: Suspected IVC tumor thrombus; contraindication to CT contrast [MS-5].

staging.rationale: To evaluate for osseous metastases; not routinely performed unless elevated serum ALP or bone pain [34, MS-4].
staging.indication: Elevated ALP or bone pain; not routine [KID-1, MS-4].

staging.rationale: To evaluate for brain metastases; brain metastases occur in 2%-8% of advanced RCC patients [233, MS-31].
staging.indication: Neurologic symptoms or signs suggesting brain metastases [KID-1, MS-4].

staging.rationale: Not a routine imaging tool to diagnose kidney cancer or follow for relapse after nephrectomy [36, MS-5]. However, FDG-PET is useful in certain circumstances including: bone-predominant disease; assessment prior to metastasectomy; FH-deficient RCC; and SDHB-deficient RCC [37, 38, MS-13, MS-31].
staging.indication: Bone-predominant disease; pre-metastasectomy assessment; FH-deficient RCC; SDHB-deficient RCC [MS-13, MS-31].

staging.rationale: Biopsy of small lesions may be considered to obtain or confirm a diagnosis of malignancy and guide decisions on surveillance, percutaneous ablation, SBRT, and/or surgery [35, KID-1]. Biopsy should also be considered if a central lesion or homogeneous infiltration of renal parenchyma is observed to rule out urothelial carcinoma or lymphoma [MS-5].
staging.indication: Small lesions where diagnosis is uncertain; central masses (to rule out urothelial carcinoma); homogeneous infiltration (to rule out lymphoma); guidance for active surveillance, ablation, or surgery [KID-1, MS-5].

biomarkers.test: Tumor histology and grade (ISUP/WHO grading system)
biomarkers.rationale: Essential for determining histologic subtype (clear cell, papillary, chromophobe, etc.) and nuclear grade (G1-G4); grade is a critical prognostic factor; grade 4 includes tumors with marked nuclear pleomorphism, multinucleate giant cells, rhabdoid, and/or sarcomatoid differentiation [ST-1, MS-4].
biomarkers.when: Obtained after surgical removal or biopsy; essential for treatment planning and prognosis [ST-1, MS-4].

biomarkers.test: Genetic evaluation for hereditary RCC syndromes
biomarkers.rationale: Criteria for further genetic risk evaluation include: personal or family history with known P/LP variant; RCC diagnosed at age ≤46 years; bilateral or multifocal tumors; ≥1 first- or second-degree relative with RCC; personal or family history of mesothelioma, uveal melanoma, GIST, pheochromocytoma, paraganglioma, cutaneous/uterine leiomyoma; histologic features associated with specific hereditary syndromes [HERED-RCC-1].
biomarkers.when: Should be considered for patients ≤46 years, those with bilateral or multifocal tumors, family history of RCC, or features suggestive of hereditary syndromes [HERED-RCC-1, MS-33].

do_not_miss: Contrast-enhanced CT abdomen with renal protocol — essential for initial characterization and staging of all renal masses [KID-1]
CT chest or chest x-ray — essential staging study; lung is most common metastatic site [KID-1, MS-4]
Core needle biopsy — when diagnosis uncertain, central mass suspected for urothelial carcinoma, or guidance for surveillance/ablation/surgery needed [KID-1, MS-5]
Genetic evaluation — for patients ≤46 years, bilateral/multifocal disease, or family history [HERED-RCC-1]
Histologic subtype and grade determination — essential for prognosis and treatment selection including adjuvant therapy eligibility (grade 4 tumors for adjuvant pembrolizumab) [ST-1, KID-2]
IVC evaluation — MRI preferred when IVC tumor involvement is suspected [MS-5]
avoid: Fine-needle aspiration (FNA) of renal masses — not adequate for histologic diagnosis; core needle biopsy is recommended instead [KID-1]

Routine FDG-PET/CT for initial diagnosis or routine post-nephrectomy surveillance — PET is not routinely recommended for these indications [36, MS-5]; it is useful only in specific circumstances (FH-deficient/SDHB-deficient RCC, bone-predominant disease, pre-metastasectomy assessment) [MS-13, MS-31]
Routine bone scan in the absence of symptoms or elevated ALP — not routinely performed unless clinically indicated [34, KID-1, MS-4]

t_stages.description: Tumor ≤7 cm in greatest dimension, limited to the kidney
Tumor ≤4 cm in greatest dimension, limited to the kidney
Tumor >4 cm but ≤7 cm in greatest dimension, limited to the kidney
Tumor >7 cm in greatest dimension, limited to the kidney
Tumor >7 cm but ≤10 cm in greatest dimension, limited to the kidney
Tumor >10 cm, limited to the kidney
Tumor extends into major veins or perinephric tissues, but not into the ipsilateral adrenal gland and not beyond Gerota's fascia
Tumor extends into the renal vein or its segmental branches, or invades the pelvicalyceal system, or invades perirenal and/or renal sinus fat but not beyond Gerota's fascia
Tumor extends into the vena cava below the diaphragm
Tumor extends into the vena cava above the diaphragm or invades the wall of the vena cava
Tumor invades beyond Gerota's fascia (including contiguous extension into the ipsilateral adrenal gland)

n_stages.description: Regional lymph nodes cannot be assessed

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Tumor ≤7 cm confined to the kidney without regional lymph node or distant metastasis; 5-year survival for localized disease is 79% (2015-2021 data) [1, MS-4]. Treatment is curative with surgery as primary approach; options include partial nephrectomy (preferred), percutaneous ablation, SBRT, active surveillance, or radical nephrectomy in select patients [KID-1].

Tumor >7 cm confined to the kidney without regional lymph node or distant metastasis; higher risk of recurrence than Stage I; radical nephrectomy is curative therapy [MS-9]; for clear cell histology, adjuvant pembrolizumab (category 1) or adjuvant belzutifan + pembrolizumab (category 2A) is an option for Grade 4 tumors with clear cell histology ± sarcomatoid features [KID-2].

stage_groups.criteria: T1-T2 with N1, M0; OR T3 with any N (NX, N0, or N1), M0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Tumor with regional lymph node involvement or tumor extending into major veins or perinephric tissues; curative therapy is radical nephrectomy [MS-9]; adjuvant pembrolizumab (category 1) or adjuvant belzutifan + pembrolizumab (category 2A) is recommended for clear cell histology [KID-2]; larger tumors have substantially higher risk of both local and metastatic recurrence [MS-13].

Tumor invades beyond Gerota's fascia without distant metastasis; nephrectomy is recommended for resectable disease; adjuvant pembrolizumab or adjuvant belzutifan + pembrolizumab is an option for clear cell histology after resection [KID-3].
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Potentially curative with aggressive multimodal approach [KID-3]

stage_groups.group: Stage IV (M1, potentially surgically resectable primary)
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Metastatic disease with potentially surgically resectable primary; systemic therapy (category 1) is now the preferred approach, with consideration of cytoreductive nephrectomy in select patients; individualized treatment based on symptoms and extent of metastatic disease [KID-3, MS-15].
Metastatic disease with surgically unresectable primary; tissue sampling to confirm diagnosis and guide management; systemic therapy is recommended [KID-3].

staging_pearls: The most important prognostic determinants of 5-year survival are the tumor stage, grade, local extent of the tumor, presence of regional nodal metastases, and evidence of metastatic disease at presentation [4-13, MS-4].
Approximately 66% of kidney cancer cases are diagnosed as localized disease, and the 5-year survival rate between 2015 and 2021 is 79% [1, MS-4].
RCC primarily metastasizes to the lung, bone, liver, lymph nodes, adrenal gland, and brain [14-16, MS-4].

For metastatic disease, the Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center (MSKCC) and International Metastatic Renal Cell Carcinoma Database Consortium (IMDC) prognostic models are used to stratify risk and guide treatment [KID-E, MS-14].

IMDC risk groups: favorable-risk (no adverse factors) — median OS not reached; intermediate-risk (1-2 factors) — median OS 27 months, 2-year OS 53%; poor-risk (3-6 factors) — median OS 8.8 months, 2-year OS 7% [113, MS-14].
MSKCC prognostic factors include: interval from diagnosis to treatment of <1 year, KPS <80%, serum LDH >1.5× ULN, corrected serum calcium > ULN, and serum hemoglobin < LLN [112, KID-E].
IMDC prognostic factors include: <1 year from diagnosis to systemic therapy, PS <80% (Karnofsky), hemoglobin < LLN, calcium > ULN, neutrophil > ULN, and platelets > ULN [113, KID-E].

Grade 4 histology (marked nuclear pleomorphism and/or multinucleate giant cells and/or rhabdoid and/or sarcomatoid differentiation) is an important prognostic factor that determines eligibility for adjuvant pembrolizumab in Stage II clear cell RCC [ST-1, KID-2].
After surgical excision of localized tumors, 20% to 30% of patients experience relapse, with a median time to relapse of 1 to 2 years and most relapses occurring within 3 years [103, 104, MS-11].
Lung metastasis is the most common site of distant recurrence, occurring in 50% to 60% of patients [103, MS-11].

management_principles.overview: The management of renal cell carcinoma (RCC) requires a multidisciplinary approach, emphasizing nephron-sparing surgery when feasible and risk stratification for adjuvant and systemic therapy decisions. Treatment intent varies from curative for localized disease to palliative for advanced stages. Active surveillance is a viable option for select patients with small renal masses and significant comorbidities. For metastatic disease, the choice of systemic therapy is guided by histology, risk stratification (MSKCC/IMDC), and prior therapy history. Supportive care and management of treatment-related toxicities are integral throughout the disease trajectory. [KID-A, KID-D]

by_intent.patient_group: Localized RCC (Stages I-III, resectable T4, M0)
by_intent.approach: Primary treatment involves surgical resection (partial or radical nephrectomy) or ablative techniques (percutaneous ablation, SBRT) for medically inoperable patients. Adjuvant pembrolizumab is recommended for clear cell histology with high-risk features (stage II grade 4 ± sarcomatoid, stage III). Neoadjuvant systemic therapy may be considered for select patients with large or locally advanced tumors to achieve cytoreduction. [KID-1, KID-2, KID-3]

by_intent.patient_group: Stage IV (M1, unresectable T4, M0) or relapsed disease
by_intent.approach: Treatment aims to prolong survival and maintain quality of life. Options include systemic therapy (first-line and subsequent lines), cytoreductive nephrectomy in selected patients, and metastasectomy or SBRT for oligometastatic disease. Best supportive care is recommended for all patients. [KID-3, KID-4]

management_principles.mdt_requirements: Management should involve a multidisciplinary team including urologic oncologists, medical oncologists, radiation oncologists, pathologists, radiologists, and palliative care specialists. Genetic counseling should be offered to patients with suspected hereditary RCC syndromes. For complex cases (e.g., inferior vena cava tumor thrombus, hereditary syndromes), referral to high-volume centers is recommended. [KID-A, HERED-RCC-1]
management_principles.performance_status_guide: Performance status (PS) influences treatment eligibility. For localized disease, patients with ECOG PS 0-1 are candidates for surgery. For cytoreductive nephrectomy, candidates generally have excellent PS (ECOG PS <2) and no brain metastasis. For systemic therapy, PS is a key factor in treatment selection and tolerability. Patients with poor PS may be better candidates for best supportive care or less intensive regimens. [KID-A, KID-3]

management_pathways.title: Management of Stage I (T1a) Renal Cell Carcinoma

nodes.criteria: Clinical stage T1a (tumor ≤4 cm, confined to kidney)

options.label: Partial nephrectomy (preferred)
options.recommendation: Category 2A. Recommended for patients with solitary kidney, bilateral tumors, chronic kidney disease, or a normal contralateral kidney. [KID-1]

options.label: Percutaneous ablation
options.recommendation: Category 2A for T1a. Suitable for patients who are not surgical candidates or prefer a less invasive approach. [KID-1]

options.label: Stereotactic body radiation therapy (SBRT)
options.recommendation: Category 2A. For non-optimal surgical candidates. Requires referral to high-volume center. [KID-1, KID-B]

options.label: Active surveillance
options.recommendation: Category 2A. For patients with small renal masses, competing risks of death, or significant comorbidities. Entails serial imaging with timely intervention if growth is noted. [KID-1]

options.label: Radical nephrectomy
options.recommendation: Category 2A. In select patients if partial nephrectomy is not technically feasible. [KID-1]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant therapy is not indicated for stage I disease.

management_pathways.title: Management of Stage I (T1b) Renal Cell Carcinoma

nodes.criteria: Clinical stage T1b (tumor >4 cm but ≤7 cm, confined to kidney)

options.label: Partial nephrectomy
options.recommendation: Category 2A. Recommended if technically feasible and provides oncologic outcomes comparable to radical nephrectomy. [KID-1]

options.label: Radical nephrectomy
options.recommendation: Category 2A. An alternative if partial nephrectomy is not feasible. [KID-1]

options.label: Active surveillance
options.recommendation: Category 2A. In select patients with competing risks. [KID-1]

options.label: Percutaneous ablation
options.recommendation: Category 2B. In select patients not eligible for surgery. [KID-1]

options.label: SBRT
options.recommendation: Category 2A. For non-optimal surgical candidates. [KID-1, KID-B]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant therapy is not indicated for stage I disease.

management_pathways.title: Management of Stage II Renal Cell Carcinoma

nodes.criteria: Stage II (T2, N0, M0), clear cell histology

options.label: Partial nephrectomy or radical nephrectomy
options.recommendation: Category 2A. Radical nephrectomy is standard; partial nephrectomy may be considered if technically feasible. [KID-2]

options.label: SBRT
options.recommendation: Category 2B. For non-optimal surgical candidates. [KID-2, KID-B]

options.label: Adjuvant pembrolizumab
options.recommendation: Category 1. For Grade 4 tumors with clear cell histology ± sarcomatoid features. [KID-2]

options.label: Adjuvant belzutifan + pembrolizumab
options.recommendation: Category 2A. For Grade 4 tumors with clear cell histology ± sarcomatoid features. [KID-2]

options.label: Surveillance
options.recommendation: Category 2A. An option if adjuvant therapy is not indicated or chosen. [KID-2]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant therapy (pembrolizumab or belzutifan + pembrolizumab) is recommended for Grade 4 tumors with clear cell histology ± sarcomatoid features.

options.label: SBRT
options.recommendation: Category 2B. For non-optimal surgical candidates. [KID-2, KID-B]

options.label: Surveillance
options.recommendation: Category 2A. Recommended as adjuvant therapy is not indicated for non-clear cell histology. [KID-2]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant therapy is not indicated for non-clear cell histology.

management_pathways.title: Management of Stage III Renal Cell Carcinoma

nodes.criteria: Stage III (T1-T2, N1 or T3, NX/N0/N1, M0), clear cell histology

options.label: Radical nephrectomy (preferred) or partial nephrectomy if clinically indicated
SBRT
options.recommendation: Category 3. For non-optimal surgical candidates. [KID-2, KID-B]

options.label: Surveillance
options.recommendation: Category 2A. An option if adjuvant therapy is not chosen. [KID-2]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant therapy (pembrolizumab or belzutifan + pembrolizumab) is recommended for clear cell histology.

options.label: Radical nephrectomy (preferred) or partial nephrectomy if clinically indicated
SBRT
options.recommendation: Category 3. For non-optimal surgical candidates. [KID-2, KID-B]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant therapy is not indicated for non-clear cell histology.

management_pathways.title: Management of Stage IV (Resectable T4, M0) Renal Cell Carcinoma

nodes.criteria: Stage IV, T4, M0, resectable, clear cell histology

options.label: Surveillance
options.recommendation: Category 2A. An option if adjuvant therapy is not chosen. [KID-3]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant therapy (pembrolizumab or belzutifan + pembrolizumab) is recommended after nephrectomy.

nodes.criteria: Stage IV, T4, M0, resectable, non-clear cell histology

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant therapy is not indicated for non-clear cell histology.

management_pathways.title: First-Line Systemic Therapy for Stage IV (M1) Clear Cell Renal Cell Carcinoma

nodes.criteria: Metastatic clear cell RCC, all risk groups

options.label: Cabozantinib (monotherapy)
options.recommendation: Category 2A for intermediate-/poor-risk. Category 2B for favorable-risk. Based on CABOSUN trial. [KID-D]

options.label: Axitinib + avelumab
options.recommendation: Category 2A. Based on JAVELIN Renal 101 trial. [KID-D]

options.label: Active surveillance
options.recommendation: Category 2A. For select, asymptomatic patients with favorable-risk disease. [KID-D]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant therapy is not applicable in the metastatic first-line setting.

management_pathways.title: Subsequent Systemic Therapy for Clear Cell Renal Cell Carcinoma

nodes.criteria: Clear cell RCC with prior IO therapy

options.label: Belzutifan
options.recommendation: Category 2A. For patients who received prior PD-1/L1 and VEGF inhibitors. [KID-D]

nodes.criteria: Clear cell RCC, IO therapy naïve

management_pathways.title: Systemic Therapy for Non-Clear Cell Renal Cell Carcinoma

options.label: Clinical trial (preferred)
options.recommendation: Category 2A. Enrollment in a clinical trial is preferred. Tumor genomic testing should be considered for trial eligibility. [KID-D]

options.label: Cabozantinib
options.recommendation: Category 2A. Based on SWOG 1500 trial for papillary RCC. [KID-D]

options.label: Erlotinib + bevacizumab
options.recommendation: Category 2A. For select patients with advanced papillary RCC, including HLRCC-associated RCC. [KID-D, HERED-RCC-D]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Not applicable in the metastatic setting.

management_pathways.title: Management of Oligometastatic Renal Cell Carcinoma

nodes.criteria: Oligometastatic disease (limited number of metastatic sites)

options.label: Metastasectomy
options.recommendation: Category 2A. For select patients with limited metastases and good extracranial disease control. [KID-4]

options.label: SBRT
options.recommendation: Category 2A. May offer durable local control. Consider for oligometastasis unless metastasectomy is planned or SBRT cannot be delivered. [KID-4, KID-B]

options.label: First-line systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Category 2A. May be considered before or after local therapy. [KID-4]

nodes.criteria: Metastasectomy with complete resection of all disease, clear cell histology

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant therapy (pembrolizumab or belzutifan + pembrolizumab) is recommended after metastasectomy with complete resection.

management_pathways.title: Management of von Hippel-Lindau (VHL) Disease-Associated RCC

nodes.criteria: Confirmed VHL disease with renal mass

options.label: Partial nephrectomy (preferred)
options.recommendation: Category 2A. Consider when tumors approach 3 cm in diameter to balance metastatic risk and renal function. Referral to high-volume center recommended. [HERED-RCC-C]

options.label: Percutaneous ablation
options.recommendation: Category 2A. For those with significant medical or surgical risk. [HERED-RCC-C]

options.label: Belzutifan
options.recommendation: Category 2A. For VHL-associated RCC not requiring immediate surgery. [HERED-RCC-D]

management_pathways.title: Management of Hereditary Leiomyomatosis and Renal Cell Cancer (HLRCC)-Associated RCC

options.label: Radical nephrectomy (preferred)
options.recommendation: Category 2A. Surveillance is not recommended due to aggressive nature. FDG-PET useful for staging. [HERED-RCC-C]

items.recommendation: Category 2A. Perform at initial presentation and for follow-up. [KID-1]

items.text: Evaluation for symptoms of metastatic disease (bone pain, neurological symptoms)
Comprehensive metabolic panel (including serum calcium, creatinine, liver function tests)
items.recommendation: Category 2A. Corrected serum calcium > ULN is a poor prognostic factor. [KID-1, KID-E]
Category 2A. >1.5 x ULN is a poor prognostic factor (MSKCC). [KID-E]

items.text: Abdomen ± pelvis CT or MRI (with and without IV contrast)
items.recommendation: Category 2A. Essential for initial workup and staging. Renal protocol preferred. [KID-1]
Category 2A. To assess for pulmonary metastases. [KID-1]
Category 2A. If clinically indicated (e.g., elevated ALP, bone pain). [KID-1, KID-C]
Category 2A. If neurological symptoms or high risk of brain metastases. [KID-1]
Category 2A. Useful in certain circumstances (e.g., bone-predominant disease, prior to metastasectomy, FH-deficient RCC, SDHB-deficient RCC). [KID-C, KID-4]
Category 2A. May be considered for small lesions to guide decisions on surveillance, ablation, and/or surgery. FNA is not adequate. [KID-1]
Category 2A. Essential for treatment selection (clear cell vs. non-clear cell). Consider tumor genomic testing for clinical trial eligibility. [KID-D]

items.text: MSKCC Prognostic Model or IMDC Criteria
items.recommendation: Category 2A. For patients with metastatic disease to stratify risk and guide systemic therapy selection. [KID-E]
Category 2A. Consider genetic evaluation if: age ≤46 years, bilateral/multifocal tumors, family history of RCC, or certain histologic features. [KID-1, HERED-RCC-1]
Category 2A. For patients meeting criteria for hereditary RCC. [HERED-RCC-1]

surgery.role: Surgical resection remains the primary curative treatment for localized RCC and a palliative option for select patients with metastatic disease. The goal is complete tumor removal while maximizing preservation of renal function when feasible.
principles: Nephron-sparing surgery (partial nephrectomy) is preferred for stage I tumors and whenever technically feasible to preserve renal function and reduce chronic kidney disease risk. [KID-A]
Radical nephrectomy is indicated for larger or locally advanced tumors (stage II, III, resectable T4) or when partial nephrectomy is not feasible. [KID-A, KID-2, KID-3]
Open, laparoscopic, or robotic techniques are acceptable; outcomes are equivalent in experienced hands. [KID-A]

Regional lymph node dissection is optional but should be considered for patients with adenopathy on imaging or palpable nodes at surgery. Extended dissection templates are provided for right- and left-sided tumors. [KID-A]
Ipsilateral adrenalectomy may be omitted if the adrenal gland is uninvolved and not high-risk. [KID-A]

Percutaneous ablation (cryosurgery, radiofrequency, microwave) is an option for clinical stage T1 lesions, particularly for non-surgical candidates. Biopsy prior to or at ablation is recommended. [KID-A, KID-1]
Stereotactic body radiation therapy (SBRT) is an option for non-optimal surgical candidates with T1 and T2a tumors. Referral to a high-volume center is recommended. [KID-A, KID-B]

procedures.name: Partial Nephrectomy (Nephron-Sparing Surgery)
procedures.indications: Stage I–III tumors when technically feasible; imperative indications (solitary kidney, chronic kidney disease, bilateral tumors); hereditary RCC syndromes. [KID-A]
procedures.key_complications: Hemorrhage, urinary leak, renal function impairment, infection.
procedures.operative_mortality: Not specified in source; generally low in high-volume centers.

procedures.name: Radical Nephrectomy
procedures.approach: Open, laparoscopic, or robotic. Includes perifascial resection of kidney, perirenal fat, and regional lymph nodes.
procedures.indications: Stage II, III, resectable stage IV (T4, M0) disease; tumor extends into inferior vena cava or involves >50% of kidney; partial nephrectomy would result in poorly functioning kidney. [KID-A, KID-2, KID-3]
procedures.key_complications: Hemorrhage, injury to adjacent organs, chronic kidney disease, cardiovascular morbidity.
procedures.operative_mortality: Can reach 10% for complex cases with caval thrombus. [KID-A]

procedures.name: Cytoreductive Nephrectomy
procedures.indications: Stage IV, M1 disease with surgically resectable primary tumor in select patients (excellent PS, no brain metastasis). [KID-3]
procedures.operative_mortality: Not specified; patient selection is critical.

procedures.name: Metastasectomy
procedures.approach: Varies by site (e.g., thoracotomy for lung, craniotomy for brain).
procedures.indications: Oligometastatic disease with controlled primary tumor and good performance status. [KID-4]

procedures.name: Percutaneous Ablation
procedures.indications: Clinical stage T1 renal lesions, particularly for non-surgical candidates. Suitable for masses ≤3 cm. [KID-A, KID-1]
procedures.key_complications: Pain, bleeding, infection, thermal injury to adjacent structures, incomplete ablation requiring retreatment.

surgery.resectability_criteria: Resectability is determined by imaging and surgical assessment. For primary tumors, complete resection (R0) should be achievable. For metastatic disease, resectability of both primary and metastatic sites is considered. Key factors include tumor size, location, involvement of adjacent structures, and presence of distant metastases. For patients with clinical T2, T3, or T4 tumors without metastases but in whom complete resection is not feasible upfront, presurgical neoadjuvant therapy may be considered following confirmation of clear cell histology to achieve cytoreduction. [KID-2, KID-3]
surgery.pre_operative_optimisation: Medical optimization (e.g., control of hypertension, diabetes, renal function assessment) and nutritional support. For hereditary syndromes, preoperative screening for pheochromocytoma (in VHL and PGL/PCC syndromes) is mandatory. [HERED-RCC-C]

radiation_therapy.role: Radiation therapy (RT) is primarily used for palliation of symptomatic metastases and as a definitive or adjuvant treatment for select patients with localized disease who are not surgical candidates. SBRT is the preferred modality for most indications due to superior local control. [KID-B]
principles: SBRT should be considered as the primary radiation modality in all situations unless precluded by anatomic site, proximity to organs at risk (OAR), or past treatments. [KID-B]
A biopsy should be obtained whenever possible prior to SBRT. [KID-B]
All patients should have a urologic evaluation. [KID-B]
Strict adherence to normal tissue constraints is mandatory to minimize toxicity. [KID-B]

For primary disease, SBRT is an option for non-optimal surgical candidates with T1 and T2a tumors (≤10 cm). When tumors abut bowel, a 5-fraction schedule with strict adherence to bowel constraints is recommended. Volumetric image guidance with motion management is required. On-table adaptive technology should be considered when available. [KID-B]
For distant metastatic disease, SBRT is preferred for oligometastases unless metastasectomy is planned or SBRT cannot be delivered. SBRT may offer more durable local control. [KID-B]

dose_frameworks.name: Primary Disease - 5 Fractions
dose_frameworks.indication: Larger tumors or when dose-volume criteria for OAR cannot be met with higher BED regimens. Every-other-day schedule may be considered to mitigate GI toxicity. [KID-B]

approaches.name: SBRT for Primary Disease
approaches.indication: Non-optimal surgical candidates with clinical stage T1a (category 2A), T1b (category 2A), stage II (category 2B), or stage III (category 3) RCC. [KID-1, KID-2, KID-B]
approaches.key_trial: TROG 15.03 FASTRACK II trial (100% local control at 12 months for T1 tumors). [KID-B]
approaches.toxicities: Mostly grade 1-2; grade 3 events (e.g., nausea, vomiting, pain) in 10% of patients. [KID-B]

approaches.name: SBRT for Distant Metastatic Disease
approaches.dose_fractionation: Spine: 16-24 Gy in 1 fraction, 24-28 Gy in 2 fractions, 24-30 Gy in 3 fractions, 30-40 Gy in 5 fractions. Other sites: 16-24 Gy in 1 fraction, 48-60 Gy in 3 fractions, 40-60 Gy in 4-5 fractions. [KID-B]
approaches.indication: Oligometastatic disease. SBRT should be considered unless metastasectomy is planned or SBRT cannot be delivered. [KID-4, KID-B]
approaches.key_trial: SABR-COMET trial (improved PFS for oligometastatic cancers treated with SBRT). [KID-B]
approaches.toxicities: Site-specific; strict adherence to OAR constraints is required to minimize toxicity. [KID-B]

approaches.name: Palliative Radiation Therapy
approaches.dose_fractionation: 8 Gy in 1 fraction, 20 Gy in 5 fractions, 30 Gy in 10 fractions. [KID-B]
approaches.indication: Symptomatic extracranial metastases. Higher doses or hypofractionated regimens may provide more durable palliation. [KID-B]

approaches.name: Stereotactic Radiosurgery (SRS) for Brain Metastases
approaches.dose_fractionation: Based on tumor size: ≤20 mm: up to 24 Gy; 21-30 mm: up to 18 Gy; 31-40 mm: up to 15 Gy. Larger tumors may be treated with fractionated SRT (e.g., 24-27 Gy in 3 fractions, 25-35 Gy in 5 fractions). [KID-B]
approaches.indication: Brain metastases. Primary treatment or adjuvant after resection. [KID-B]

systemic_therapy.overview: Systemic therapy for advanced RCC includes immune checkpoint inhibitor (ICI) combinations with tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs), ICI monotherapy, and TKI monotherapy. Treatment selection is guided by histology (clear cell vs. non-clear cell), risk stratification (MSKCC/IMDC for clear cell), and prior therapy history. For non-clear cell RCC, clinical trials are preferred, and tumor genomic testing is recommended. [KID-D]

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Adjuvant therapy for localized clear cell RCC (high-risk)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Based on KEYNOTE-564 trial showing improved disease-free survival and overall survival. [KID-2]
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: Clear cell histology, high-risk features (stage II grade 4 ± sarcomatoid, stage III). [KID-2]
by_stage_and_setting.contraindications: Not specified in adjuvant setting; see general ICI precautions.

by_stage_and_setting.setting: First-line therapy for metastatic clear cell RCC
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Axitinib + pembrolizumab, cabozantinib + nivolumab, ipilimumab + nivolumab, lenvatinib + pembrolizumab (all category 1). Cabozantinib monotherapy is category 2A preferred for intermediate-/poor-risk. [KID-D]
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Multiple phase III trials demonstrate superior efficacy over sunitinib. [KID-D]
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Axitinib + avelumab, pazopanib, sunitinib (all category 2A other recommended). Active surveillance for select favorable-risk patients. [KID-D]
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: Clear cell histology. Risk stratification (MSKCC/IMDC) informs but does not limit choice for most ICI combinations. Cabozantinib monotherapy preference for intermediate-/poor-risk. [KID-D, KID-E]
by_stage_and_setting.contraindications: Avoid combination with cabozantinib + nivolumab if prior ICI therapy (category 2B for subsequent use). [KID-D]

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Subsequent therapy for metastatic clear cell RCC (prior IO therapy)
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Axitinib, belzutifan, cabozantinib, everolimus/lenvatinib, tivozanib (all category 2A other recommended). [KID-D]
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Based on trials like METEOR, LITESPARK-005, TIVO-3. Belzutifan is specifically for patients who received prior PD-1/L1 and VEGF inhibitors. [KID-D]
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: IO/TKI combinations (category 2B useful in certain circumstances) due to limited post-IO data. [KID-D]
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: Clear cell histology. For belzutifan, prior PD-1/L1 and VEGF inhibitor therapy. [KID-D]
by_stage_and_setting.contraindications: Avoid re-challenge with IO combinations after prior IO therapy (based on CONTACT-03 and TiNivo2 trials). [KID-D]

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Subsequent therapy for metastatic clear cell RCC (IO therapy naïve)
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Axitinib + pembrolizumab, cabozantinib, cabozantinib + nivolumab, everolimus/lenvatinib, ipilimumab + nivolumab, lenvatinib + pembrolizumab, nivolumab (all category 2A other recommended). [KID-D]
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Based on various trials; some data pre-date widespread IO use. [KID-D]
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Axitinib, everolimus, lenvatinib, pazopanib, sunitinib, tivozanib (category 2A useful in certain circumstances). [KID-D]

by_stage_and_setting.setting: First-line therapy for metastatic non-clear cell RCC
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Clinical trial (category 2A preferred), cabozantinib, cabozantinib + nivolumab, lenvatinib + pembrolizumab (all category 2A preferred). [KID-D]
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Response rates to targeted therapy are lower than in clear cell RCC; trials are preferred. [KID-D]
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Erlotinib + bevacizumab (for HLRCC/papillary), everolimus/lenvatinib, ipilimumab + nivolumab, nivolumab, pembrolizumab, sunitinib (category 2A other recommended). [KID-D]
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: Histologic subtype confirmation. Consider tumor genomic testing for trial eligibility. [KID-D]
by_stage_and_setting.contraindications: For renal medullary carcinoma (RMC), immune checkpoint therapies (e.g., ipilimumab + nivolumab, pembrolizumab monotherapy) should be avoided outside of a clinical trial due to limited efficacy and potential for rapid progression. Platinum-based chemotherapy is preferred. [KID-D]

key_regimens.name: Ipilimumab + Nivolumab
key_regimens.cycle_days: 4 cycles of combination, then nivolumab maintenance.
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Immune-mediated adverse events (e.g., colitis, hepatitis, pneumonitis). [KID-D]

key_regimens.name: Cabozantinib (monotherapy)
key_regimens.setting: First-line metastatic clear cell RCC (intermediate-/poor-risk) or subsequent therapy.

key_regimens.name: Belzutifan
key_regimens.setting: Subsequent therapy for metastatic clear cell RCC (prior PD-1/L1 and VEGF inhibitors).

key_regimens.name: Erlotinib + Bevacizumab
key_regimens.setting: Metastatic HLRCC-associated RCC or sporadic papillary RCC.

key_regimens.name: Everolimus (for TSC-associated angiomyolipoma)
key_regimens.setting: Tuberous sclerosis complex (TSC) with angiomyolipoma not requiring immediate surgery.

key_regimens.name: Platinum-based chemotherapy (e.g., gemcitabine/carboplatin)
key_regimens.setting: Renal medullary carcinoma (RMC) or collecting duct carcinoma.

treatment_response_assessment.title: Assessment of Treatment Response and Disease Surveillance
treatment_response_assessment.timing: Response assessment is performed periodically during systemic therapy, typically every 6-16 weeks based on treatment schedule and clinical status. For localized disease, surveillance follows stage-specific guidelines. [KID-C, KID-4]
response_logic: For patients on systemic therapy, imaging (CT or MRI with and without contrast) is performed at baseline and every 6-16 weeks to assess response per RECIST criteria. Interval may be adjusted based on rate of disease change and sites of active disease. [KID-C, KID-4]
For localized disease post-treatment, surveillance imaging is based on stage and treatment type (e.g., active surveillance, ablation, nephrectomy, SBRT). [KID-C]
Clinical assessment includes history, physical examination, and laboratory evaluation as per treatment requirements. [KID-C, KID-4]
imaging_recommendations: Abdominal imaging (CT or MRI with and without contrast) is the primary modality for assessing renal and local recurrence. [KID-C]
Chest CT is used to evaluate for pulmonary metastases. [KID-C]
Brain MRI is considered if neurological symptoms or risk of brain metastases. [KID-C, KID-4]
Bone scan or bone survey as clinically indicated for bone pain or elevated alkaline phosphatase. [KID-C, KID-4]
FDG-PET is useful in certain circumstances (e.g., bone-predominant disease, prior to metastasectomy, FH-deficient RCC, SDHB-deficient RCC). [KID-C, KID-4]
biopsy_or_salvage_logic: If imaging suggests residual or recurrent disease after local therapy (e.g., ablation), biopsy or further treatment may be indicated. [KID-C]
For progression on systemic therapy, subsequent therapy options are considered based on prior treatments and histology. [KID-D]
Tissue biopsy should be obtained at recurrence when feasible to confirm histology and guide therapy, especially for non-clear cell subtypes or clinical trial eligibility. [KID-4, KID-D]

prognosis.overall: Kidney cancer prognosis varies significantly by stage and histology. Approximately 66% of cases are diagnosed as localized disease, with an overall 5-year survival rate of 79% (2015-2021 data). The most important prognostic determinants of 5-year survival are tumor stage, grade, local extent of the tumor, presence of regional nodal metastases, and evidence of metastatic disease at presentation. RCC primarily metastasizes to the lung, bone, liver, lymph nodes, adrenal gland, and brain.

by_stage.five_yr_survival: Not explicitly stated; overall localized disease 5-year survival is 79%.
by_stage.context: Patients with localized tumors have a good prognosis. After surgical excision, 20% to 30% of patients with localized tumors experience relapse. Lung metastasis is the most common site of distant recurrence, occurring in 50% to 60% of patients. The median time to relapse after surgery is 1 to 2 years, with most relapses occurring within 3 years.

These patients have a substantially higher risk of both local and metastatic recurrence, which warrants an increased follow-up frequency. The most important prognostic determinants of 5-year survival are the tumor stage, grade, local extent of the tumor, presence of regional nodal metastases, and evidence of metastatic disease at presentation.

by_stage.five_yr_survival: Not explicitly stated; median overall survival varies by risk group.
by_stage.context: The International Metastatic Renal Cell Carcinoma Database Consortium (IMDC) model stratifies patients into risk groups with different median OS: Favorable-risk (no adverse factors): median OS not reached, 2-year OS 75%; Intermediate-risk (1-2 factors): median OS 27 months, 2-year OS 53%; Poor-risk (3-6 factors): median OS 8.8 months, 2-year OS 7%.

prognostic_factors: Evidence of metastatic disease at presentation
Laboratory markers (LDH, hemoglobin, calcium, neutrophil count, platelet count)
Histologic subtype (clear cell vs. non-clear cell)
Presence of sarcomatoid or rhabdoid features
prognosis.prognostic_context: The Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center (MSKCC) Prognostic Model and the International Metastatic Renal Cell Carcinoma Database Consortium (IMDC) Criteria are used to risk-stratify patients with metastatic RCC and guide treatment selection. The MSKCC model uses five factors: interval from diagnosis to treatment <1 year, Karnofsky PS <80%, serum LDH >1.5x ULN, corrected serum calcium > ULN, and serum hemoglobin < LLN. The IMDC model adds two factors to the MSKCC model: absolute neutrophil count > ULN and platelets > ULN. These models help predict survival and determine appropriateness of certain therapies.

disease_related.complication: Metastatic disease (lung, bone, liver, lymph nodes, adrenal gland, brain)
disease_related.management: Systemic therapy, surgery (metastasectomy), radiation therapy (SBRT), or active surveillance depending on volume, location, and symptoms.
Bone-modifying agents (denosumab or bisphosphonates like zoledronic acid), palliative radiation therapy (SBRT preferred), and pain management.

disease_related.complication: Brain metastases (occurs in ~2-8% of advanced RCC)
disease_related.management: Stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) or fractionated stereotactic radiation therapy (SRT) for limited metastases; whole-brain radiation therapy (WBRT) only for palliative purposes when SRS/SRT is not feasible.

immunotherapy: Immune-mediated adverse events (colitis, hepatitis, pneumonitis, endocrinopathies, rash)

supportive_care.overview: Supportive care is a mainstay of therapy for all patients with metastatic RCC and should be integrated throughout the disease course. The goal is to manage symptoms, treatment-related toxicities, and improve quality of life. The NCCN Guidelines for Palliative Care should be consulted.
supportive_care.nutritional_support: Nutritional assessment and support are recommended as needed to manage weight loss, anorexia, or treatment-related gastrointestinal side effects (e.g., diarrhea, stomatitis).
supportive_care.anti_emetic_protocol: Anti-emetic prophylaxis and treatment should be based on the emetogenic potential of the systemic therapy regimen used, following anti-emetic guidelines.
supportive_care.gcsf_guidance: Granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF) support should be considered for regimens with a high risk of febrile neutropenia or in patients with risk factors.
supportive_care.vte_prophylaxis: Venous thromboembolism (VTE) prophylaxis is recommended for high-risk patients, including those with active cancer, immobilization, or prior VTE history. Treatment with VEGFR-TKIs may increase hypertension and bleeding risk, requiring careful assessment.
supportive_care.pain_management: Pain should be assessed and managed according to the NCCN Guidelines for Adult Cancer Pain. This includes pharmacologic (analgesics, adjuvants) and non-pharmacologic approaches. Radiation therapy, particularly SBRT, is recommended for painful bone metastases.
supportive_care.psychosocial_support: Psychosocial assessment and support should be offered to address distress, anxiety, depression, and quality of life concerns. Referral to social work, psychology, or palliative care teams is recommended.
supportive_care.dental_care: Dental evaluation prior to starting bone-modifying agents (bisphosphonates, denosumab) is recommended to prevent osteonecrosis of the jaw. Good oral hygiene and avoidance of invasive dental procedures during treatment are advised.

follow_up.post_curative_treatment: Follow-up after curative treatment for localized RCC (e.g., nephrectomy, ablation, SBRT) is individualized based on stage, histology, and risk of recurrence. It includes history and physical (H&P), laboratory tests, and imaging (abdominal and chest CT). The frequency and duration of follow-up may be extended beyond 5 years at the clinician's discretion, as late recurrences can occur.
follow_up.surveillance_rationale: The rationale for surveillance is to detect recurrence or metastatic disease early, when it may still be amenable to curative or life-prolonging interventions (e.g., metastasectomy, SBRT). It also allows monitoring of renal function and treatment-related side effects. The frequency is risk-stratified, with higher-risk patients (e.g., stage III, high-grade) requiring more intensive follow-up.
late_effects_screening: Chronic kidney disease: Monitor renal function (serum creatinine, eGFR) regularly, especially after nephrectomy.
Cardiovascular disease: Monitor blood pressure and manage cardiovascular risk factors, as both RCC and some treatments (VEGFR-TKIs) can increase cardiovascular risk.
Recurrence or metachronous tumors: Continued abdominal and chest imaging to detect local recurrence, contralateral kidney tumors, or pulmonary metastases.
follow_up.recurrence_patterns: Most recurrences occur within the first 3-5 years after surgery, but late recurrences (>5 years) are possible. Lung metastases are the most common site of distant recurrence (50-60%), followed by bone, lymph nodes, liver, and brain. Local recurrence rates are lower for smaller tumors after partial nephrectomy (1.4-2%) versus larger tumors (10%).

clinical_follow_up_schedule.schedule: H&P and laboratory tests annually. Abdominal CT/MRI within 3-12 months, then annually for up to 5 years or longer. Chest CT annually for at least 5 years.
H&P and laboratory tests annually. Abdominal/pelvic CT/MRI every 6 months for 2 years, then annually for up to 5 years. Chest CT annually for at least 5 years.

H&P and comprehensive metabolic panel every 3-6 months for 3 years, then annually for up to 5 years. Abdominal/pelvic CT/MRI every 3-6 months for at least 3 years, then annually for up to 5 years. Chest CT every 3-6 months for at least 3 years, then annually for up to 5 years. Additional imaging (bone scan, brain MRI) as symptoms warrant.

H&P every 6-16 weeks during systemic therapy. Imaging (CT/MRI of chest, abdomen, pelvis) every 6-16 weeks, adjusted based on disease status and treatment schedule. MRI of head at baseline and as clinically indicated. Bone scan/survey as clinically indicated.

surveillance.imaging_strategy: Imaging with and without IV contrast (e.g., renal protocol CT) is strongly preferred for abdomen evaluation. Chest CT is preferred over chest x-ray for higher sensitivity. MRI is an alternative if contrast is contraindicated. FDG-PET may be useful in specific circumstances (e.g., bone-predominant disease, pre-metastasectomy assessment, FH-deficient or SDHB-deficient RCC).
laboratory_monitoring: Comprehensive metabolic panel (including serum creatinine, calcium, liver function tests)
Monitoring specific to systemic therapy (e.g., thyroid function for lenvatinib/pembrolizumab, liver function for pazopanib)
supportive_follow_up: Assessment of performance status and quality of life
Management of treatment-related toxicities (e.g., hypertension, diarrhea, hand-foot syndrome)
Monitoring for immune-mediated adverse events during immunotherapy
Renal function monitoring, especially after nephrectomy or with nephrotoxic therapies

special_situations.situation: Hereditary RCC Syndromes (e.g., VHL, HLRCC, BHDS, TSC, HPRC, PGL/PCC, BAP1-TPDS)
special_situations.key_message: Patients with suspected or confirmed hereditary RCC should be referred for genetic evaluation and counseling. Kidney-specific surveillance, surgical, and systemic therapy recommendations vary by syndrome.
special_situations.management: Genetic testing, syndrome-specific surveillance (e.g., annual abdominal MRI for HLRCC starting age 8-10), nephron-sparing surgery when feasible (BHDS, HPRC, TSC), radical nephrectomy for HLRCC, and targeted therapies (e.g., belzutifan for VHL, erlotinib+bevacizumab for HLRCC).
special_situations.evidence: Based on NCCN expert consensus and available literature. For VHL, belzutifan showed ORR of 49% in LITESPARK-004. For HLRCC, erlotinib+bevacizumab showed ORR of 72% in a phase II trial.

special_situations.key_message: Clinical trials are preferred for nccRCC. Tumor genomic testing should be considered. Treatment options include cabozantinib, cabozantinib+nivolumab, lenvatinib+pembrolizumab, erlotinib+bevacizumab (for papillary/HLRCC), and others.
special_situations.management: Cabozantinib is a category 2A, preferred option. Cabozantinib+nivolumab and lenvatinib+pembrolizumab are also preferred. For HLRCC, erlotinib+bevacizumab is other recommended. For renal medullary carcinoma, platinum-based chemotherapy is preferred.
special_situations.evidence: SWOG 1500: Cabozantinib improved PFS vs. sunitinib in papillary RCC (HR 0.60). KEYNOTE-B61: Lenvatinib+pembrolizumab ORR 49% in untreated nccRCC. Phase II: Erlotinib+bevacizumab ORR 72% in HLRCC.

special_situations.key_message: Local ablative therapies (metastasectomy, SBRT, percutaneous ablation) can be considered for oligometastatic disease in selected patients.
special_situations.management: Metastasectomy with complete resection followed by adjuvant pembrolizumab (within 1 year) or belzutifan+pembrolizumab. SBRT or percutaneous ablation for oligometastases if metastasectomy is planned or not feasible.
special_situations.evidence: Prospective phase II studies support SBRT for oligometastases, postponing systemic therapy. Long-term relapse-free survival has been reported in selected patients.

special_situations.key_message: Cytoreductive nephrectomy may be considered in select patients with good performance status, no brain metastasis, and surgically resectable primary tumor. Patient selection is key.
special_situations.management: Individualize based on symptoms, extent of metastatic disease, and risk group. The CARMENA trial showed sunitinib alone was non-inferior to sunitinib after nephrectomy in patients with metastatic RCC, but a post-hoc analysis suggested benefit for those with only one IMDC risk factor.
special_situations.evidence: CARMENA trial: sunitinib alone vs. nephrectomy+sunitinib: median OS 18.4 vs. 13.9 months (HR 0.89). Ongoing trials (SEVURO-CN, PROBE, NORDIC-SUN) are evaluating cytoreductive nephrectomy in the IO era.

special_situations.key_message: RMC is an aggressive subtype almost exclusively in patients with sickle-cell trait/disease. Platinum-based chemotherapy is the preferred first-line therapy.
special_situations.management: Avoid immune checkpoint inhibitors outside clinical trials as they may cause rapid progression. Preferred regimens: gemcitabine/carboplatin, paclitaxel/carboplatin, or gemcitabine/cisplatin. Doxorubicin/gemcitabine can also produce responses.
special_situations.evidence: Nze CN et al. 2023 and Soeung M et al. 2025 showed limited efficacy and potential for rapid progression with ICI in RMC.

clinical_pearls: Partial nephrectomy (nephron-sparing surgery) is preferred for stage I tumors when feasible, as it provides comparable oncologic outcomes to radical nephrectomy while preserving renal function and reducing cardiovascular risk.
Adjuvant pembrolizumab is a category 1 recommendation for patients with intermediate- or high-risk clear cell RCC after nephrectomy, based on the KEYNOTE-564 trial showing improved DFS and OS.

For metastatic clear cell RCC, first-line combination regimens (axitinib+pembrolizumab, cabozantinib+nivolumab, ipilimumab+nivolumab, lenvatinib+pembrolizumab) are category 1 preferred options across all risk groups.
Risk stratification using MSKCC or IMDC criteria is essential for guiding treatment selection and predicting prognosis in metastatic RCC.

Active surveillance may be an option for select patients with small renal masses (<2 cm), T1a tumors, or those with significant comorbidities. For metastatic RCC, active surveillance can be considered in select asymptomatic patients with favorable-risk disease.
SBRT is a viable alternative for non-optimal surgical candidates with stage I (T1a, T1b) or stage II RCC, and for palliation of oligometastatic disease.
Genetic evaluation is recommended for patients with RCC diagnosed at ≤46 years, bilateral/multifocal tumors, or a family history of RCC, as ~3% of RCC cases are hereditary.
For non-clear cell RCC, cabozantinib is a preferred first-line option, and clinical trial enrollment is strongly encouraged due to limited data compared to clear cell histology.

guidelines_resources.title: IMDC Criteria for Prognostic Risk Stratification
guidelines_resources.notes: Used to stratify patients with metastatic RCC into favorable, intermediate, and poor risk groups based on six clinical parameters.
